basalt-run----component--annotation.name: _Component Annotation
basalt-run----component--annotation.description: _Component Annotation labels elements with contextual metadata positioned relative to their anchor, using directional placement and visual hierarchy to guide attention without disrupting layout.

basalt.usage: Use annotations to highlight new features, progressive disclosure hints, or validation states where inline text would create cognitive load. In onboarding flows, annotations direct users toward primary actions; in forms, they clarify field requirements or errors without displacing content. Position determines content priority: top/bottom for primary guidance, left/right for secondary clarification.
basalt.never: Don't stack multiple annotations on a single element — cognitive load multiplies and layout becomes fragile. Use Secondary variants for primary information instead. Use Primary or Group variants to layer communication hierarchy within constrained space.
basalt.a11y: Annotations must have role="tooltip" or role="status" depending on whether they supplement interactive controls (tooltip) or announce dynamic state changes (status), with aria-describedby linking to the annotated element.

basalt-run----component--note.name: _Component Note
basalt-run----component--note.description: _Component Note surfaces contextual information or warnings at the point of relevance within a page or form.

basalt.usage: Use _Component Note when you need to highlight important context that affects a user's decision or action, such as deprecation notices in API documentation or inline validation warnings during form completion. Choose this component over modals or tooltips when the information should remain persistently visible and integrated with surrounding content.
basalt.never: Don't use _Component Note for errors that prevent form submission — use dedicated error states on the field itself instead. Reserve _Component Note for advisory information that allows the user to proceed with full context.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="status" with aria-live="polite" to announce updates to assistive technologies without interrupting user flow. Use aria-label to provide a descriptive prefix like 'Warning:' when the icon alone doesn't convey intent.

basalt-run----slot.name: .Slot
basalt-run----slot.description: .Slot renders a designated container that anchors supplementary content within a layout.

basalt.usage: Use .Slot when you need a consistent, predictable location for optional or contextual information—such as help text beneath a form field, metadata in a data table row, or additional actions in a card footer. Choosing .Slot ensures spacing and typography remain system-compliant without custom sizing logic.
basalt.never: Don't nest multiple .Slots directly inside each other — this creates unclear visual hierarchy and redundant spacing layers. Use a single .Slot with multiple child elements or separate semantic containers instead.
basalt.a11y: Ensure the parent container has role="region" with an aria-label when .Slot holds landmark content; otherwise it carries no implicit ARIA role and must inherit context from its parent.

basalt-run--accordion.name: Accordion
basalt-run--accordion.description: Accordion organizes content into collapsible sections, revealing details on demand.

basalt.usage: Use Accordion when you need to reduce cognitive load by hiding secondary information beneath expandable headers—common in FAQs, settings panels, and documentation. Choose this over tabs when the relationship between sections is hierarchical rather than parallel, or when space is constrained and only one section needs to be visible at a time.
basalt.never: Don't nest Accordions more than one level deep — it creates navigation friction and breaks scanability. Use Tabs instead if users need to compare content across sections simultaneously.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="button" on each trigger with aria-expanded set to true or false. Ensure keyboard users can expand/collapse with Enter or Space and navigate between triggers with arrow keys per ARIA Authoring Practices Guide 1.2.

basalt-run--accordion--item.name: Accordion Item
basalt-run--accordion--item.description: Accordion Item presents a collapsible section with a trigger header and expandable content area.

basalt.usage: Use Accordion Item when organizing related content into expandable sections, particularly in settings panels, FAQs, or feature comparisons where progressive disclosure reduces cognitive load. Group multiple items within a parent Accordion component to create a cohesive, keyboard-navigable interface. Width and layout are determined by the parent container; individual items should not set fixed dimensions.
basalt.never: Don't nest Accordion Items within other Accordion Items — this breaks keyboard navigation and creates confusing focus management. Use a Tab component or Stepper instead for multi-level hierarchies.
basalt.a11y: Implement the button role on the trigger header with aria-expanded set to true when open, false when closed. Pair with aria-controls referencing the content region's id. Support Enter and Space keys to toggle the expanded state.

basalt-run--a-i-----conversation.name: AI -> Conversation
basalt-run--a-i-----conversation.description: AI -> Conversation renders a structured message exchange between user and assistant within a contained, scrollable context.

basalt.usage: Use this component to display multi-turn AI interactions where message history, attribution, and message differentiation matter. Deploy in chat interfaces, AI-assisted workflows, or documentation that surfaces AI-generated content alongside user queries. The component maintains visual hierarchy between speaker roles and supports both short queries and lengthy responses without layout collapse.
basalt.never: Don't nest multiple conversation threads within a single Conversation component — it obscures message flow and violates linear conversation semantics. Use separate Conversation instances or a Conversation.Thread subcomponent instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="log" on the message container to announce new messages to screen readers as they arrive; set aria-live="polite" to avoid interrupting ongoing speech. Ensure message order in DOM matches chronological order in visual presentation (WCAG 1.3.2).

basalt-run--a-i--chat-----chat--response.name: AI Chat -> Chat Response
basalt-run--a-i--chat-----chat--response.import: import { AI Chat -> Chat Response } from "@your-org/design-system";
basalt-run--a-i--chat-----chat--response.description: AI Chat -> Chat Response renders a message block containing an icon, text content, and visual separators for AI-generated replies in conversational interfaces.

basalt.usage: Use this component to display responses from an AI assistant within a chat thread. Employ it when you need visual consistency between multiple AI messages, such as in customer support workflows, product documentation interfaces, or multi-turn conversation experiences. The fixed styling ensures legibility and establishes clear separation between user and assistant messages.
basalt.never: Don't use this component for user-generated messages — it signals AI origin through its visual treatment. Use Chat User Message instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="article" on the component root and wrap text content with role="log" with aria-live="polite" to announce new responses to assistive technology users.

basalt-run--a-i--chat-----code--block.name: AI Chat -> Code Block
basalt-run--a-i--chat-----code--block.import: import { AI Chat -> Code Block } from "@your-org/design-system";
basalt-run--a-i--chat-----code--block.description: AI Chat -> Code Block renders syntax-highlighted code snippets within conversational contexts, supporting copy and language identification.

basalt.usage: Use this component when an AI assistant generates executable code, configuration files, or code examples in chat. This appears frequently in developer-focused AI tools where users need to quickly understand and extract code. Pair with copy-to-clipboard affordances when users are expected to use the code outside the chat context.
basalt.never: Don't render unsanitized code directly from user input — XSS vulnerabilities can execute arbitrary scripts. Use a code sanitization library or escape all user-generated content before rendering.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="region" with aria-label="code block" and aria-live="polite" to announce code to screen readers. Ensure the copy button has aria-label="Copy code" and communicates success with aria-live="assertive".

basalt-run--a-i--chat-----user-message.name: AI Chat -> User message
basalt-run--a-i--chat-----user-message.import: import { AI Chat -> User message } from "@your-org/design-system";
basalt-run--a-i--chat-----user-message.description: AI Chat -> User message renders a user's query or statement within a conversational interface, anchored to the trailing edge with supporting visual hierarchy.

basalt.usage: Use this component whenever capturing user input within a chat context, particularly in AI assistant interfaces where message attribution matters. Deploy in customer support chats, AI product demos, or multi-turn conversation flows where visual distinction from system messages is essential. The component's right-aligned positioning and secondary background establish clear message ownership without relying on color alone.
basalt.never: Don't apply primary or accent colors to the background — secondary background ensures sufficient contrast for readability and visual hierarchy. Use Background.Default.Secondary instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="article" with aria-label="User message: [message content]" to ensure screen reader users can distinguish user messages from system responses in the conversation flow.

basalt-run--a-i--chat--box.name: AI Chat Box
basalt-run--a-i--chat--box.description: AI Chat Box surfaces conversational AI interactions as a contained, persistent interface element within application layouts.

basalt.usage: Deploy this component when your product integrates AI assistance as a secondary feature rather than a primary experience—think support chat in a dashboard or contextual help in an editor. It anchors AI functionality to specific contexts without consuming full-screen real estate. Prefer this over full-page chat experiences when users need to maintain parallel focus on other content.
basalt.never: Don't nest Chat Box within modals or overlays that already constrain viewport space — this creates competing containment hierarchies and degrades readability. Use a full-screen Chat interface or inline text input instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="region" with aria-label="AI Assistant" and ensure all messages receive aria-live="polite" to announce incoming responses to screen readers without interrupting user focus.

basalt-run--a-i--chatbot.name: AI Chatbot
basalt-run--a-i--chatbot.description: AI Chatbot delivers conversational experiences through a message interface with real-time message exchange, input controls, and conversation history.

basalt.usage: Deploy this component for customer support, product guidance, or task automation where conversational interaction reduces friction. Use on product pages to answer frequently asked questions without page navigation, or embed in dashboards for on-demand data retrieval through natural language. The conversation persists across user sessions when connected to a backend that maintains history.
basalt.never: Don't render messages without sender attribution (user vs. assistant) — users lose context and trust in multi-turn conversations. Use the Message component with explicit role designation instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role='log' on the message container with aria-live='polite' to announce new assistant messages to screen readers without interrupting user input (WCAG 4.1.3 Status Messages).

basalt-run--a-i--sidebar.name: AI Sidebar
basalt-run--a-i--sidebar.description: AI Sidebar presents contextual AI assistance in a collapsible vertical panel anchored to the viewport edge.

basalt.usage: Use AI Sidebar when providing secondary AI features that shouldn't interrupt primary content or require persistent access. Deploy in documentation interfaces, code editors, or content creation tools where users toggle AI help on-demand. Reserve for experiences where the sidebar can occupy screen real estate without compromising the main task.
basalt.never: Don't auto-expand the sidebar on page load — users lose context of their primary workflow. Use manual trigger patterns like toggle buttons instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="complementary" with aria-label="AI Assistant" and ensure keyboard focus can enter/exit the sidebar via Tab, with Escape closing it when open (WCAG 2.1 Level AA keyboard accessibility).

basalt-run--avatar.name: Avatar
basalt-run--avatar.description: Avatar displays a user's identity through an image or a single initial character within a defined shape and size.

basalt.usage: Use Avatar to represent individual users in lists, navigation contexts, or anywhere identity recognition matters. Deploy the image variant when profile photos are available; use initials when images are missing or uploading. In team rosters or comment threads, Avatar provides visual continuity and reduces cognitive load.
basalt.never: Don't use multiple initials (e.g., 'AA' or 'JDo') — character width forces overflow and breaks the container. Use a single initial or the image variant instead.
basalt.a11y: Set role="img" and provide an aria-label with the full user name (e.g., aria-label="Alice Johnson") so screen readers announce identity, not just a visual mark.

basalt-run--avatar--block.name: Avatar Block
basalt-run--avatar--block.description: Avatar Block combines a user avatar with associated metadata in a compact, scannable unit.

basalt.usage: Use Avatar Block when displaying user identity alongside contextual information like status, role, or timestamp. This component appears in sidebars, user lists, comment threads, and team rosters where visual identification paired with secondary details reduces cognitive load. Prefer this over separate avatar and text elements when the metadata is inseparable from the identity context.
basalt.never: Don't truncate names without visual indication — users cannot determine if content is cut off. Use text truncation with ellipsis or a tooltip instead.
basalt.a11y: Avatar image must have an alt attribute describing the user; the entire block should have role="listitem" when part of a user list (WCAG 1.1.1). The secondary text must use Text.Default.Tertiary token to maintain sufficient color contrast ratio of 4.5:1 for WCAG AA compliance.

basalt-run--avatar--group.name: Avatar Group
basalt-run--avatar--group.description: Avatar Group displays a collection of user avatars in a compact, visual layout.

basalt.usage: Use Avatar Group when representing multiple participants, contributors, or team members in a confined space—such as in comment threads, collaboration headers, or member lists. This component reduces visual clutter while maintaining recognizability through overlapping or spaced arrangements. Reserve it for contexts where individual avatar identity matters less than collective presence.
basalt.never: Don't display more than five avatars without a count indicator — visual overflow obscures individual recognition and creates cognitive load. Use an overflow badge with numeric count instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="group" on the container and provide an accessible label via aria-label describing the collective purpose (e.g., 'Contributors to this project'), ensuring screen reader users understand context without enumerating each avatar.

basalt-run--button.name: Button
basalt-run--button.description: Button triggers an action or navigation when activated.

basalt.usage: Use Button for primary actions within forms, dialogs, and page-level interactions. Choose variants based on action hierarchy—Primary for main calls-to-action like submitting a payment, Neutral for secondary actions like canceling, Subtle for tertiary affordances like "Learn more" links within dense interfaces.
basalt.never: Don't use Button for navigation between pages—use Link instead. Buttons trigger state changes; links change location. Confusing these breaks user expectations and analytics.
basalt.a11y: Button must have role="button" or use semantic <button> element, support keyboard activation via Space and Enter keys per WCAG 2.1 SC 2.1.1, and expose accessible text via visible label or aria-label.

basalt-run--button--danger.name: Button Danger
basalt-run--button--danger.description: Button Danger communicates destructive actions with high visual prominence through saturated color and clear affordance.

basalt.usage: Use Button Danger for irreversible actions—account deletion, permanent data removal, or destructive state changes. Reserve it for actions requiring explicit user intent; pair with confirmation dialogs when consequences are severe. Primary variant suits modal footers and inline deletions; Subtle variant works in tables or secondary positions where context already signals danger.
basalt.never: Don't use Button Danger for recoverable actions like undo or draft discard — use Button Secondary instead. Destructive visual treatment creates cognitive friction for non-destructive operations.
basalt.a11y: Ensure button element has aria-label="Delete [specific item]" or equivalent label text that names the action; avoid generic "Delete" on multiple buttons in sequence (WCAG 2.1 2.4.3).

basalt-run--button--group.name: Button Group
basalt-run--button--group.description: Button Group surfaces a set of related actions in a single visual container with consistent spacing and alignment.

basalt.usage: Use Button Group when presenting 2–4 related actions that deserve equal visual weight, such as filter toggles, sort options, or confirmation choices. Within a modal footer, Button Group clarifies the relationship between primary and secondary actions. Avoid grouping unrelated actions or mixing interaction patterns within a single group.
basalt.never: Don't use Button Group for navigation between pages — use Tabs or a navigation menu instead. Button Group clarifies immediate context-specific actions, not persistent navigation structure.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="group" on the container and ensure all buttons within the group remain keyboard focusable with Tab. Logical tab order must reflect visual left-to-right or top-to-bottom layout. Each button retains its own accessible name via text content or aria-label.

basalt-run--calendar.name: Calendar
basalt-run--calendar.description: Calendar displays a grid-based date picker for selecting single or multiple dates within a bounded timeframe.

basalt.usage: Use Calendar when users need to select dates with full-month context, such as booking appointments, filtering reports by date range, or scheduling events. It's particularly valuable when the specific day of the week matters to your users' decision-making. Pair it with a text input for inline date entry when space or interaction patterns demand lighter-weight alternatives.
basalt.never: Don't allow users to select dates outside the component's min/max bounds without clear visual indication — users will be confused about why certain dates appear disabled. Use a disabled state with supporting helper text instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="application" on the calendar container and ensure all date cells are keyboard-navigable with arrow keys per WCAG 2.1 Level AA, with Tab only reaching the previous/next month controls to prevent excessive tabbing.

basalt-run--calendar--button.name: Calendar Button
basalt-run--calendar--button.description: Calendar Button triggers date selection or navigation within a date picker interface.

basalt.usage: Use Calendar Button for individual date cells in a calendar grid or for range selection endpoints. Deploy in scheduling interfaces, booking flows, or analytics dashboards where users need to select specific dates. Avoid custom date inputs when Calendar Button provides native affordance and consistent interaction.
basalt.never: Don't use Calendar Button for non-date actions — it establishes user expectation for temporal selection. Use Button component instead.
basalt.a11y: Calendar Button must have role="button" and aria-pressed="true|false" to communicate selection state to assistive technologies. Keyboard navigation requires arrow keys to move between dates and Enter to select (WCAG 2.1 2.1.1).

basalt-run--calendar--month--field.name: Calendar Month Field
basalt-run--calendar--month--field.description: Calendar Month Field captures and displays a single month and year selection through an interactive date picker.

basalt.usage: Use Calendar Month Field when users need to select a month for filtering, scheduling, or reporting within a bounded time context. Common in financial dashboards, invoice date ranges, and subscription billing cycles. Pair with adjacent day or time fields only when the full date granularity is necessary.
basalt.never: Don't use Calendar Month Field to capture full dates with day precision — the component intentionally omits day selection. Use Date Picker instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="combobox" with aria-expanded toggling the calendar popup state. Users must navigate months with arrow keys (Left/Right for previous/next month) and confirm selection with Enter.

basalt-run--calendar--select--group.name: Calendar Select Group
basalt-run--calendar--select--group.description: Calendar Select Group presents a set of date range selection controls with a unified visual container and shared interaction state.

basalt.usage: Use Calendar Select Group when users need to select a contiguous date range within a single interaction context, such as filtering analytics reports by date or setting appointment availability windows. This component consolidates multiple date inputs into a cohesive unit, reducing cognitive load compared to separate input fields.
basalt.never: Don't use Calendar Select Group for non-contiguous date selection — the component is designed specifically for continuous ranges and will confuse users attempting to select multiple separate dates. Use Calendar Multi-Select instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="group" on the container and associate each date input with a descriptive label using aria-labelledby; keyboard navigation must support Tab between inputs and arrow keys within calendar pickers (WCAG 2.1 level AA, 2.1.1).

basalt-run--calendar--year--field.name: Calendar Year Field
basalt-run--calendar--year--field.description: Calendar Year Field captures a single year value through a focused, date-scoped input designed for year-only selection.

basalt.usage: Use Calendar Year Field when filtering datasets by year, setting fiscal periods, or collecting birth years where month and day are irrelevant. In financial dashboards, this component reduces cognitive load compared to full date pickers. Pair it with adjacent month or day fields only when the complete date is necessary.
basalt.never: Don't use Calendar Year Field to capture full dates — the component intentionally omits day and month selection. Use Calendar Picker or a full date input instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="combobox" with aria-expanded, aria-controls, and aria-haspopup="listbox" to expose the dropdown behavior. Keyboard users must navigate the year list with Arrow Up/Down and confirm selection with Enter (WCAG 2.1 Level AA, 2.1.1 Keyboard).

basalt-run--card.name: Card
basalt-run--card.description: Card containers group related content with consistent spacing, typography, and optional borders.

basalt.usage: Use cards to organize content into scannable, modular units—such as product listings, settings panels, or data summaries. Cards work best when the parent container defines width constraints; set auto layout to 'fill' to let content flow naturally within its bounds.
basalt.never: Don't nest cards within cards to create hierarchy — use sections or dividers within a single card instead. Use Stroke variant to add visual separation only when the card sits on a colored or image-heavy background.
basalt.a11y: Card containers must have role='region' with aria-label describing their content purpose when they function as distinct information landmarks.

basalt-run--card---slot-.name: Card (Slot)
basalt-run--card---slot-.description: Card (Slot) wraps content in a contained surface with consistent spacing, border, and corner radius.

basalt.usage: Use Card (Slot) to group related information or actions into visually distinct sections. Common applications include dashboard panels, form containers, and modal bodies where content needs clear visual separation from surrounding elements. Reserve this component for layouts where the contained content benefits from explicit boundary definition.
basalt.never: Don't nest Card (Slot) components within each other for visual hierarchy — this creates redundant borders and spacing logic that complicates layout math. Use a combination of Card (Slot) with section headings or dividers instead.
basalt.a11y: Ensure Card (Slot) has a semantic landmark role (main, section, or region) when it contains primary page content; apply role="region" with aria-label when the slot purpose isn't visually obvious.

basalt-run--card--grid--content--list.name: Card Grid Content List

stories.name: Desktop
stories.snippet: const Desktop = () => <Card Grid Content List variant="desktop" />;

stories.name: Mobile
stories.snippet: const Mobile = () => <Card Grid Content List variant="mobile" />;

basalt-run--card--grid--content--list.import: import { Card Grid Content List } from "@your-org/design-system";
basalt-run--card--grid--content--list.description: Card Grid Content List displays structured information across multiple cards in a responsive grid layout with consistent spacing, typography, and visual hierarchy.

basalt.usage: Use Card Grid Content List when presenting collections of related items that benefit from visual separation and scannable layouts—product catalogs, feature comparisons, or team directories. It provides better information density than linear lists while maintaining readability across screen sizes. Choose this over single-column layouts when users need to evaluate multiple items simultaneously.
basalt.never: Don't mix content types or hierarchy levels within a single grid—cards for pricing tiers shouldn't appear alongside cards for testimonials. Use separate Card Grid Content Lists for each content category instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="list" on the grid container and role="listitem" on each card. Ensure card titles are semantic heading elements (h2 or h3) and maintain a logical tab order through interactive elements with keyboard navigation support.

basalt-run--card--grid--icon.name: Card Grid Icon
basalt-run--card--grid--icon.description: Card Grid Icon displays a grid of icon-labeled cards that represent distinct categories, actions, or navigation destinations.

basalt.usage: Use Card Grid Icon to present a set of related options when users need to scan and select quickly, such as product categories, feature modules, or dashboard sections. This component works well in onboarding flows, settings panels, or homepage layouts where visual hierarchy and touch targets matter equally. Avoid dense information architectures where each card requires supporting description text.
basalt.never: Don't place more than eight cards in a single grid — cognitive load increases and scanning becomes inefficient. Use Card Grid Icon with descriptions or labels that exceed two lines of text instead.
basalt.a11y: Each card must have role="button" with an accessible name via aria-label or visible text, and support keyboard navigation with Tab to focus and Enter/Space to activate (WCAG 2.1 Level AA, 2.1.1 Keyboard).

basalt-run--card--grid--image.name: Card Grid Image
basalt-run--card--grid--image.description: Card Grid Image displays a collection of image-based cards in a responsive grid layout with consistent spacing and visual hierarchy.

basalt.usage: Use Card Grid Image when presenting multiple related items that benefit from visual context, such as product galleries, portfolio collections, or feature highlights. The grid adapts responsively across viewports while maintaining card integrity and readability. Choose this component when image prominence drives user decision-making or exploration.
basalt.never: Don't use Card Grid Image for single items or linear sequences — it creates unnecessary visual complexity. Use Card or a dedicated single-item layout instead.
basalt.a11y: Each card must have an accessible name via aria-label or semantic heading (WCAG 2.1 Level A). Cards must be keyboard navigable with tab order following visual left-to-right, top-to-bottom reading pattern.

basalt-run--card--grid--pricing.name: Card Grid Pricing
basalt-run--card--grid--pricing.description: Card Grid Pricing displays multiple pricing tiers in a responsive grid layout with visual hierarchy and call-to-action elements.

basalt.usage: Use Card Grid Pricing to present subscription plans, product editions, or feature-based pricing options. This component works best when comparing 2–4 tiers with distinct value propositions, such as SaaS pricing pages or tiered feature access. Grid layout automatically adapts to viewport, reducing cognitive load for plan comparison across devices.
basalt.never: Don't place more than four pricing cards in a single grid — visual comparison becomes ineffective and layout degrades. Use Pricing Table instead for detailed feature-by-feature comparisons.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="region" with aria-label="Pricing plans" on the grid container, and ensure each card's call-to-action button has aria-pressed or aria-current="page" when selected to communicate current plan state to screen readers per WCAG 2.1 Level AA.

basalt-run--card--grid--reviews.name: Card Grid Reviews
basalt-run--card--grid--reviews.description: Card Grid Reviews displays customer testimonials in a responsive grid layout with consistent visual hierarchy and spacing.

basalt.usage: Use Card Grid Reviews to showcase social proof on landing pages, product pages, or case study sections where credibility matters. Deploy on homepages to highlight customer satisfaction metrics, or on pricing pages to reinforce purchasing decisions through authentic user feedback. This component works best when you have 3-12 reviews to display without overwhelming the layout.
basalt.never: Don't truncate review text without a clear read-more affordance — users need complete context to evaluate credibility. Use Expandable Card or full-page review section instead.
basalt.a11y: Each review card must have role="article" with a semantic heading (h3 or h4) identifying the reviewer, and the grid container requires role="region" with aria-label="Customer reviews" for screen reader context per WCAG 1.3.1.

basalt-run--card--grid--testimonials.name: Card Grid Testimonials
basalt-run--card--grid--testimonials.description: Card Grid Testimonials displays customer quotes in a responsive grid layout with consistent spacing and typography hierarchy.

basalt.usage: Use this component to showcase customer validation on marketing pages, case studies, or product landing pages. Choose it when you need to present multiple testimonials with equal visual weight while maintaining scannable content. The grid layout scales efficiently across breakpoints without reflow complexity.
basalt.never: Don't truncate testimonial text with ellipsis to force fit content — this obscures the customer's full message and reduces credibility. Use Text.Default.Secondary for attribution instead.
basalt.a11y: Each card must have role="article" with an associated heading (h2–h4) to establish proper landmark structure for screen reader navigation (WCAG 2.1 Level AA 1.3.1).

basalt-run--checkbox--field.name: Checkbox Field
basalt-run--checkbox--field.description: Checkbox Field captures binary or indeterminate selection states within forms and lists.

basalt.usage: Use Checkbox Field when users need to select multiple independent options from a set, such as filtering product categories or acknowledging terms. Pair with a label to clarify the selection's meaning. Avoid for mutually exclusive choices—use Radio Field instead.
basalt.never: Don't use Checkbox Field for single yes/no decisions in critical flows — use Toggle instead. Toggles signal immediate state changes; checkboxes signal selections pending form submission.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="checkbox" with aria-checked set to true, false, or mixed. Support Space and Enter keys to toggle state. Label must be programmatically associated via htmlFor attribute (WCAG 1.3.1).

basalt-run--checkbox--group.name: Checkbox Group
basalt-run--checkbox--group.description: Checkbox Group presents multiple related binary options in a single, contained form control.

basalt.usage: Use Checkbox Group when users need to select any combination of related items—filtering search results, permission selection in settings, or toggling multiple features simultaneously. Prefer this over individual checkboxes scattered across a layout when the options form a semantic unit. Reserve for 2–8 items; beyond that, consider a different pattern.
basalt.never: Don't mix checkbox and radio inputs in the same group — users expect uniform selection behavior within a grouped control. Use Radio Group when only one selection is valid.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="group" on the container with aria-labelledby pointing to the group label. Each checkbox must have role="checkbox", aria-checked reflecting state, and keyboard support for Space to toggle and arrow keys to navigate between items (WCAG 2.1 Level AA).

basalt-run--date--input--field.name: Date Input Field
basalt-run--date--input--field.description: Date Input Field captures a single date value through keyboard entry or native picker.

basalt.usage: Use when users need to select a specific date within a form, such as booking a flight, scheduling an appointment, or setting a deadline. Prefer this over custom date pickers when native browser support suffices, as it reduces implementation complexity and respects platform conventions.
basalt.never: Don't use as a date range selector — it accepts one date only. Use Date Range Picker instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="textbox" with aria-label="[field context, e.g., 'Departure date']" and aria-invalid="true" when displaying error state, per WCAG 4.1.2.

basalt-run--date--picker--field.name: Date Picker Field
basalt-run--date--picker--field.description: Date Picker Field captures a single date input with optional calendar interaction through the Calendar component.

basalt.usage: Use Date Picker Field when users need to select a specific date, such as setting a meeting time, entering a birth date, or filtering results by date range. Pair with Calendar for inline selection or as a popover trigger. Choose this over a text input when date validation and format consistency matter.
basalt.never: Don't use Date Picker Field to capture time-of-day information — that requires a separate Time Picker component. Use Calendar instead if the interaction needs to display an entire month view without a text input fallback.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="combobox" with aria-expanded toggling the Calendar visibility, and ensure keyboard users can navigate the field with Tab and open the Calendar with Enter or Space (WCAG 2.1 4.1.2).

basalt-run--dialog.name: Dialog
basalt-run--dialog.description: Dialog presents focused content in a modal container with a scrim overlay, blocking interaction with underlying page content.

basalt.usage: Use Dialog for confirmations, forms, or information that requires user attention before proceeding. Examples: deleting an account, selecting options during checkout, or reviewing terms before submission. Prefer Dialog over inline forms when the action is significant or blocking.
basalt.never: Don't nest Dialogs within Dialogs — cognitive load increases and navigation becomes unclear. Use a multi-step Dialog or sequential workflow instead.
basalt.a11y: Dialog must have role="dialog" and aria-modal="true", with focus trapped inside the dialog until closed. Pressing Escape must close the dialog (WCAG 2.1 Level A).

basalt-run--dialog--body.name: Dialog Body
basalt-run--dialog--body.description: Dialog Body contains the primary content of a modal or sheet overlay, constrained to 600px maximum width on card variants and full width on sheet variants.

basalt.usage: Use Dialog Body to structure the scrollable content area within dialogs. On desktop, card dialogs work well for forms, confirmations, and focused tasks. On mobile, consider sheet dialogs that anchor to the bottom and use minimum 160px height to remain usable on small screens. Add horizontal padding to card dialogs on screens under 768px to prevent full-bleed edges.
basalt.never: Don't nest scrollable containers within Dialog Body — enable scrolling on the body itself instead. Use Sheet variant for full-width overlays on mobile, not Card, which constrains content and may feel cramped on small devices.
basalt.a11y: Dialog Body must be wrapped by a parent element with role="dialog" and aria-labelledby pointing to the Dialog Header. Focus must be managed so the first interactive element receives focus on open, and focus trap must prevent tabbing outside the dialog until close.

basalt-run--examples----home--page.name: Examples / Home Page
basalt-run--examples----home--page.description: Examples / Home Page showcases product capabilities through curated use cases and interactive demonstrations at entry points in the user journey.

basalt.usage: Use this component to reduce time-to-value for new users and provide concrete proof points for feature adoption. Deploy on landing pages or onboarding flows where users need to understand what's possible before diving into the product. Include real workflows your customers actually perform—not generic placeholder scenarios.
basalt.never: Don't populate this component with abstract or overly simplified examples — users struggle to see themselves in generic use cases. Use Product-Specific Examples instead, tailored to your user's actual workflows and industry context.
basalt.a11y: Ensure all interactive demonstrations have keyboard navigation support (Tab, Enter, Escape) and use role="region" with aria-label to identify distinct example sections for screen reader users.

basalt-run--examples--about.name: Examples/About
basalt-run--examples--about.description: Examples/About presents contextual information about a product, feature, or concept through structured visual hierarchy and supporting imagery.

basalt.usage: Use this component to introduce new features during onboarding, explain core product value propositions, or provide educational context within workflows. Deploy on landing pages, help sections, or feature announcement surfaces where users benefit from narrative framing before taking action.
basalt.never: Don't nest Examples/About within modals or overlays that restrict viewport context — users lose spatial orientation and the component's visual breathing room becomes cramped. Use a dedicated page or full-width section instead.
basalt.a11y: Heading hierarchy must be explicit (h1–h6) with no skipped levels; the primary heading must have role='heading' with aria-level matching its semantic tag (WCAG 2.1 level AA, 1.3.1 Info and Relationships).

basalt-run--examples--a-i--chat.name: Examples/AI Chat
basalt-run--examples--a-i--chat.description: Examples/AI Chat renders a conversational interface displaying alternating user and assistant messages with contextual styling and action affordances.

basalt.usage: Deploy this component when presenting AI-generated responses alongside user queries in real-time chat contexts, such as customer support workflows, product documentation assistants, or multi-turn AI interactions. Use it to establish clear visual hierarchy between human and machine-generated content without requiring custom message styling.
basalt.never: Don't render user and assistant messages with identical visual treatment — it obscures conversation flow and confuses message ownership. Use distinct background and text token bindings to differentiate speaker roles.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="log" on the message container and aria-live="polite" to announce new messages to screen readers; ensure keyboard navigation moves focus through messages sequentially using Tab, with Enter activating any message-level actions.

basalt-run--examples--article.name: Examples/Article
basalt-run--examples--article.description: Examples/Article displays curated content snippets with visual hierarchy, metadata, and contextual actions for discovery and engagement.

basalt.usage: Use Examples/Article when presenting editorial content, case studies, or resource collections where visual prominence and scannability drive user engagement. Deploy across landing pages, knowledge bases, or content-rich dashboards where users benefit from seeing multiple items in sequence. The component bridges between headlines and full articles, reducing cognitive load while preserving decision-making context.
basalt.never: Don't remove the border treatment to achieve visual separation — it signals containment and prevents content bleed into adjacent sections. Use Border.Default.Default instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="article" on the root container and ensure the primary content heading uses semantic h2 or h3 (WCAG 2.1 1.3.1 Info and Relationships). The interactive element must be keyboard-accessible with visible focus state.

basalt-run--examples--contact--us.name: Examples/Contact Us
basalt-run--examples--contact--us.description: Examples/Contact Us surfaces contact information and communication channels with structured layout and visual hierarchy.

basalt.usage: Deploy this component on landing pages, help sections, or footer areas where users need immediate access to support channels. Use it when consolidating email, phone, and form submission options into a single scannable region. Choose this over scattered contact links to establish trust and reduce friction in finding support.
basalt.never: Don't mix multiple contact methods without clear visual separation — cognitive load increases and users miss alternatives. Use separate sections or Card components instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="region" with aria-label="Contact information" on the container; ensure phone numbers and email addresses are semantic links (href="tel:" and href="mailto:") for assistive technology recognition.

basalt-run--examples--landing--page.name: Examples/Landing Page
basalt-run--examples--landing--page.description: Examples/Landing Page demonstrates a complete, production-ready hero section layout with integrated navigation, call-to-action, and content hierarchy for conversion-focused entry points.

basalt.usage: Deploy this component when establishing primary web presence or onboarding flows where first impression directly impacts conversion. Use it for product launches, marketing sites, or authenticated application entry pages. The layout adapts seamlessly across viewports while maintaining information hierarchy and interaction affordances.
basalt.never: Don't nest multiple call-to-action buttons at equal visual weight — it fragments user intent and increases cognitive load. Use a primary/secondary button hierarchy instead.
basalt.a11y: The primary call-to-action button must have `role="button"` and be keyboard accessible via Tab navigation with visible focus state meeting WCAG 2.1 AA contrast ratio of 4.5:1.

basalt-run--examples--portfolio.name: Examples/Portfolio
basalt-run--examples--portfolio.description: Examples/Portfolio displays a curated grid of work samples with visual previews, metadata, and interaction affordances.

basalt.usage: Use this component to showcase a collection of projects, case studies, or portfolio pieces where visual hierarchy and scanability matter. Deploy on portfolio sites, agency homepages, or team pages where prospects or collaborators need to evaluate quality and range of work quickly. Choose this over simple lists when visual context directly influences decision-making.
basalt.never: Don't truncate project titles without affordance for expansion — users cannot assess work relevance from partial information. Use a truncation component with tooltip or full-text reveal instead.
basalt.a11y: Each portfolio item must have role="article" with an accessible name via aria-label or heading that uniquely identifies the project. Keyboard users must navigate through items via Tab with focus visible on all interactive targets (WCAG 2.1 Level AA).

basalt-run--examples--pricing.name: Examples/Pricing
basalt-run--examples--pricing.description: Examples/Pricing displays tiered pricing options with feature comparisons and call-to-action elements.

basalt.usage: Use this component on pricing pages, product marketing sites, or within onboarding flows to help users understand plan differences and make purchase decisions. Deploy on pages where plan selection directly influences conversion, such as signup flows or tier upgrade modals. The component handles feature comparison matrices and pricing hierarchy in a single, scannable layout.
basalt.never: Don't remove the visual hierarchy between plan tiers — users need immediate recognition of recommended or popular plans. Use Background.Brand.Tertiary and Icon.Brand.On Brand Secondary tokens to establish distinction instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="region" on the pricing container with aria-labelledby pointing to a heading, and ensure each plan card uses semantic <button> elements for CTAs with clear, action-oriented text like "Subscribe to Pro" rather than generic "Select".

basalt-run--examples--product--detail--p.name: Examples/Product Detail Page
basalt-run--examples--product--detail--p.description: Examples/Product Detail Page demonstrates a complete e-commerce product layout with image gallery, specifications, pricing, and purchase controls.

basalt.usage: Reference this component when building product detail experiences that require multi-image presentation, specification hierarchies, and conversion-focused calls to action. Use Desktop variant for web applications and Mobile variant for responsive experiences below 768px breakpoints. This pattern proves especially valuable for commerce platforms handling both commodity and specialty products.
basalt.never: Don't place multiple primary CTAs at equal visual hierarchy — reduces conversion clarity. Use a single Primary button for the dominant action (Add to Cart) with Secondary buttons for supporting actions (Add to Wishlist).
basalt.a11y: Implement role="region" with aria-label="Product details" on the main container and role="tablist" on image gallery thumbnails with keyboard navigation via arrow keys (WCAG 2.1 Level AA criterion 2.1.1).

basalt-run--examples--shop.name: Examples/Shop
basalt-run--examples--shop.description: Examples/Shop displays a grid-based product catalog with visual hierarchy, pricing, and call-to-action affordances for e-commerce contexts.

basalt.usage: Use Examples/Shop when building storefronts, product listing pages, or marketplace browsing experiences where discovery and rapid comparison matter. Deploy on product category pages where customers need to evaluate multiple items simultaneously, such as apparel collections or digital goods catalogs. The component balances information density with visual clarity to support both scanning and detailed inspection.
basalt.never: Don't remove the product image from the card layout — imagery is the primary decision signal in e-commerce and its absence significantly increases cognitive load. Use Card or custom layouts without Examples/Shop instead.
basalt.a11y: Each product card must have role="article" with an accessible name via aria-label or heading that includes the product name, price, and availability status to provide sufficient context without requiring visual inspection.

basalt-run--examples--slot.name: Examples/Slot
basalt-run--examples--slot.description: Examples/Slot renders a designated container for displaying contextual content, examples, or supplementary information alongside primary interface elements.

basalt.usage: Use Examples/Slot when you need to showcase code samples, usage patterns, or reference content without disrupting the main content flow. Pair it with form fields or documentation sections to provide immediate, relevant context—such as displaying API request/response examples next to integration instructions.
basalt.never: Don't nest Examples/Slot within modal dialogs — modals already constrain context, and nested slots create cognitive overload. Use a dedicated sidebar or separate documentation panel instead.
basalt.a11y: Ensure Examples/Slot has role="region" with aria-label describing its purpose (e.g., aria-label="Code example") so screen reader users understand its function within the page structure.

basalt-run--examples--waitlist.name: Examples/Waitlist
basalt-run--examples--waitlist.description: Examples/Waitlist captures email addresses and optional metadata from users before a product becomes available.

basalt.usage: Deploy this component when launching a new product, feature, or service that requires staged access. Use it to build anticipation and gather a qualified audience pool. Common scenarios include beta programs, limited releases, or pre-order campaigns where early interest signals business value.
basalt.never: Don't auto-submit the form on blur — users may accidentally trigger submission while still deciding whether to provide their email. Use explicit submit buttons instead.
basalt.a11y: The email input must have type="email" and associate a visible label element via htmlFor binding to meet WCAG 2.1 Level AA. Tab order must proceed sequentially from email input through any optional fields to the submit button.

basalt-run--footer.name: Footer
basalt-run--footer.description: Footer organizes secondary navigation, legal links, and company information at the bottom of a page or application.

basalt.usage: Use Footer to provide consistent access to tertiary actions, compliance documents, and contact details without interrupting primary content. Place it on every page of your application to establish site structure—users expect it at the bottom of documentation sites, marketing pages, and dashboard applications.
basalt.never: Don't nest interactive components deeper than two levels in Footer — it creates cognitive overhead and breaks keyboard navigation patterns. Use Disclosure or Accordion instead.
basalt.a11y: Declare the contentinfo landmark role (role="contentinfo") once per page; implement keyboard navigation to all links with Tab and allow focus to be trapped within Footer sections when navigating backward from the last link.

basalt-run--form---slot-.name: Form (Slot)
basalt-run--form---slot-.description: Form (Slot) structures grouped form fields with consistent spacing, borders, and visual containment.

basalt.usage: Use Form (Slot) to organize related inputs—such as billing address fields or account settings—into a scannable, contained section. This component prevents visual fragmentation when multiple form sections exist on a single page. Apply it when field relationships benefit from spatial grouping rather than sequential presentation.
basalt.never: Don't nest Form (Slot) components within one another — nesting creates redundant visual hierarchy and confuses field grouping intent. Use separate Form (Slot) instances at the same level instead.
basalt.a11y: Wrap the slot content with role="group" and associate it with a heading using aria-labelledby to establish semantic relationship between the group label and its fields (WCAG 1.3.1).

basalt-run--form--contact.name: Form Contact
basalt-run--form--contact.description: Form Contact displays contact information with optional icons and semantic structure for addresses, phone numbers, or email details.

basalt.usage: Use Form Contact to present contact details within forms, modals, or information sections where visual hierarchy and consistent spacing improve scannability. This component is particularly useful in checkout flows, account settings, or support contact cards where structured contact data needs clear presentation.
basalt.never: Don't nest Form Contact components — each instance should represent a single contact entry. Use separate Form Contact components or a list container instead.
basalt.a11y: Form Contact must include a semantic landmark role or heading hierarchy (ARIA role='region' or within a <section>) when used as a standalone contact block to ensure screen reader users understand its purpose and can navigate past it efficiently.

basalt-run--form--forgot--password.name: Form Forgot Password
basalt-run--form--forgot--password.description: Form Forgot Password initiates account recovery by capturing and validating a user's email address.

basalt.usage: Deploy this component on login pages when users cannot access their account credentials. Use it as the primary recovery mechanism for self-service password resets, reducing support burden for account access issues. Include it prominently within authentication flows where users encounter login failures.
basalt.never: Don't send password reset links to unverified email addresses — this creates account takeover vectors and violates OWASP authentication guidelines. Use email verification before link delivery instead.
basalt.a11y: The email input must have role="textbox" with aria-label="Email address" and aria-required="true". The submit button requires aria-disabled state management and must announce loading state changes via aria-live="polite" to screen readers.

basalt-run--form--log--in.name: Form Log In
basalt-run--form--log--in.description: Form Log In combines email and password inputs with authentication submission into a single, self-contained component.

basalt.usage: Use Form Log In at application entry points where credential-based authentication is required. This component pairs well with password reset and sign-up flows in financial platforms, SaaS tools, or admin dashboards. When authentication is optional or federated through third parties, consider pairing it with alternative authentication methods outside this component.
basalt.never: Don't disable the submit button during form submission — this removes affordance for the loading state. Use a loading spinner inside the button with aria-busy="true" instead.
basalt.a11y: The form must have role="form" with aria-label="Log in" or equivalent. Both input fields require associated labels with for attributes. The submit button must be keyboard accessible and trigger submission on Enter within either input field.

basalt-run--form--newsletter.name: Form Newsletter
basalt-run--form--newsletter.description: Form Newsletter captures email addresses and manages subscription state with integrated validation and submission feedback.

basalt.usage: Deploy this component on landing pages, blog footers, or onboarding flows where converting casual visitors into subscribers is the primary conversion goal. Use it when you need to establish trust through a minimal, focused interaction that doesn't interrupt user context. The compact footprint makes it ideal for above-the-fold real estate where attention is scarce.
basalt.never: Don't auto-subscribe users without explicit consent — this violates GDPR and CAN-SPAM regulations, eroding trust immediately. Use a checkbox or double opt-in pattern instead.
basalt.a11y: The input field must have an associated label element with for/id binding, and the submit button must have role="button" with aria-label="Subscribe" to clarify intent for screen reader users.

basalt-run--form--register.name: Form Register
basalt-run--form--register.description: Form Register captures user identity and account creation data through structured input fields with validation feedback.

basalt.usage: Use Form Register when onboarding new users or creating authenticated accounts. This component handles the standard registration flow with email, password, and profile information collection. Choose it over custom form implementations when you need consistent validation patterns and error states across your application.
basalt.never: Don't remove the password confirmation field — users make typos that create account lockout friction. Use a dedicated password confirmation input instead.
basalt.a11y: Form Register must implement role="form" with associated <label> elements for all inputs and aria-invalid="true" on fields with validation errors per WCAG 3.3.1.

basalt-run--form--shipping.name: Form Shipping
basalt-run--form--shipping.description: Form Shipping collects and validates delivery address and method information.

basalt.usage: Use Form Shipping in checkout flows where customers specify where and how their order arrives. This component consolidates address entry, shipping method selection, and estimated delivery timing into a single structured form. It's essential in e-commerce contexts where shipping decisions directly impact order fulfillment and customer expectations.
basalt.never: Don't allow form submission without validating all required address fields — incomplete shipping data causes fulfillment errors and customer support escalations. Use AddressValidator component for real-time validation instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="group" on the form container with aria-labelledby pointing to a visible heading; ensure all address inputs have associated labels and keyboard navigation follows logical tab order (address fields before method selection).

basalt-run--header.name: Header
basalt-run--header.description: Header anchors page hierarchy and surface primary navigation, identity, and actions in a fixed or sticky container.

basalt.usage: Use Header as the primary navigation container for your application or website. Place it at the top of the viewport to establish navigation hierarchy and provide consistent access to core actions like account settings or search. On marketing sites, Header typically contains logo, navigation links, and CTA buttons; in applications, it includes product navigation, user menu, and feature toggles.
basalt.never: Don't nest interactive components (buttons, links, dropdowns) more than two levels deep in Header — keyboard navigation and screen reader announcement becomes ambiguous. Use a Dropdown or Popover component to contain tertiary actions instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="banner" on the Header element and ensure all interactive children maintain focus visibility with a minimum 3:1 contrast ratio against Background.Default.Default (WCAG 2.1 Level AA). Keyboard users must navigate all Header controls using Tab and Shift+Tab without trapping focus.

basalt-run--header--auth.name: Header Auth

stories.name: Logged In - Hover
stories.snippet: const LoggedIn-Hover = () => <Header Auth variant="logged in - hover" />;

basalt-run--header--auth.description: Header Auth displays authentication state and provides access to account controls in the page header.

basalt.usage: Use Header Auth in primary navigation to surface sign-in entry points for unauthenticated users and account menus for authenticated users. Include this component on every public page where authentication state needs visibility, such as marketing sites or dashboards where users may arrive without an active session.
basalt.never: Don't open authentication flows in modals or overlays triggered from Header Auth — users expect navigation to a dedicated authentication page or OAuth flow that maintains context. Use a standard Link component to a /signin route instead.
basalt.a11y: Header Auth must implement role="button" with aria-expanded="true|false" on the account menu trigger to communicate interaction state to assistive technology. The menu container requires role="menu" with proper focus management per WCAG 2.1 Level AA.

basalt-run--hero---slot-.name: Hero (Slot)
basalt-run--hero---slot-.description: Hero (Slot) renders a full-width introductory section anchoring page identity and primary call-to-action above the fold.

basalt.usage: Use Hero (Slot) at the top of marketing pages, product launches, or documentation homepages where immediate context and navigation clarity matter most. Deploy it to establish visual hierarchy and guide users toward the next critical action—signing up, exploring features, or reading core content. On internal tools or dashboards, avoid this component; a compact header with sticky navigation serves those contexts better.
basalt.never: Don't nest interactive elements deeper than one level within the slot content — cognitive load and tap target sizing both degrade. Use a structured Button or Link component at the surface level instead.
basalt.a11y: Set role="region" aria-label="Page introduction" on the Hero container; ensure the primary call-to-action button receives focus-visible styling and keyboard navigation follows DOM order (WCAG 2.1 Level AA, 2.4.3 Focus Order).

basalt-run--hero--actions.name: Hero Actions
basalt-run--hero--actions.description: Hero Actions displays a set of primary and secondary call-to-action buttons positioned below headline content.

basalt.usage: Use Hero Actions to guide users toward immediate next steps on landing pages, empty states, or modal introductions. Deploy on product overview pages where a single primary action (sign up, start trial) paired with a secondary option (learn more, view demo) reduces friction. Prioritize this pattern when conversion clarity matters more than content hierarchy.
basalt.never: Don't stack more than two actions vertically on desktop — it dilutes visual hierarchy and confuses intent. Use Button Groups instead.
basalt.a11y: Actions must have role='group' with aria-label describing their collective purpose (e.g., 'Getting started actions'), and each button must have accessible text that works out of context per WCAG 2.1 Success Criterion 2.4.4.

basalt-run--hero--basic.name: Hero Basic
basalt-run--hero--basic.description: Hero Basic establishes visual hierarchy at the top of a page with a large title, supporting text, and optional call-to-action.

basalt.usage: Use Hero Basic to anchor landing pages, product launches, or section introductions where immediate context matters. It's most effective when the primary message can be stated in a single, concise headline—avoid using it for routine page headers or secondary content areas.
basalt.never: Don't nest interactive elements within the title or supporting text — this obscures focus management and complicates keyboard navigation. Use Button or Link components in dedicated action slots instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement the title with role="banner" and ensure heading hierarchy starts with <h1>. The heading must be the first keyboard-focusable element when the page loads (WCAG 2.4.3 Focus Order).

basalt-run--hero--form.name: Hero Form
basalt-run--hero--form.description: Hero Form combines a prominent call-to-action section with an embedded form, anchoring conversion workflows at the top of a page or modal.

basalt.usage: Use Hero Form when a single action drives significant business value and warrants dedicated above-the-fold real estate—newsletter signups, early access requests, or account creation. The form elevation reduces friction by eliminating scroll and competing content. Avoid burying conversion forms below secondary messaging when conversion is the primary page objective.
basalt.never: Don't nest multiple independent forms within a single Hero Form — splitting focus undermines conversion intent. Use separate Hero Form instances or a Stepper component instead.
basalt.a11y: The form must have a semantically correct <form> role with an associated <legend> element for the fieldset; submit buttons require aria-label when relying on icon-only presentation (WCAG 2.1 1.1.1).

basalt-run--hero--image.name: Hero Image
basalt-run--hero--image.description: Hero Image displays a full-width visual container with optional overlay text, serving as the primary visual anchor for a page or section.

basalt.usage: Use Hero Image at the top of landing pages, product showcases, or campaign pages where a prominent visual statement establishes context. Deploy it when imagery carries equal narrative weight to accompanying copy—such as a homepage featuring a product in-environment or a case study opening with client-specific photography. Avoid using Hero Image as decorative filler; the image itself should communicate value.
basalt.never: Don't place actionable buttons directly over the image without a scrim overlay — Text.Utilities.Text On Overlay with Background.Utilities.Scrim ensures sufficient contrast for WCAG AA compliance. Use the scrim token instead.
basalt.a11y: Hero images must include descriptive alt text that conveys narrative intent, not just object identification. For images containing embedded text overlays, include overlay text in the alt attribute or as visible caption text; do not rely on the image file alone to communicate critical information (WCAG 1.1.1 criterion).

basalt-run--hero--newsletter.name: Hero Newsletter
basalt-run--hero--newsletter.description: Hero Newsletter combines prominent messaging with an email capture form to drive subscription conversions at critical user journeys.

basalt.usage: Deploy this component at page entry points where conversion intent is highest—landing pages, post-article surfaces, or campaign destinations. Use when newsletter signup represents a primary conversion goal rather than a secondary call-to-action. The component works best above the fold to maximize visibility before scroll.
basalt.never: Don't nest multiple form fields beyond email—conversion rates drop with additional required inputs. Use a single email input with optional secondary messaging instead.
basalt.a11y: The form must have role="region" with aria-label="Newsletter signup" and the submit button must announce loading state via aria-busy="true" during submission (WCAG 4.1.3).

basalt-run--icon--button.name: Icon Button
basalt-run--icon--button.description: Icon Button triggers an action or navigation when pressed, using only an icon without accompanying text.

basalt.usage: Reach for Icon Button in space-constrained contexts like toolbars, headers, or floating action buttons where a text label would reduce scannability. Use Primary for critical actions like submit or delete, Neutral for standard interactions like opening a menu, and Subtle for secondary or toggle states like favorites. In a document editor toolbar, Primary starts a save, Neutral opens font options, and Subtle toggles bold formatting.
basalt.never: Don't use Icon Button without a visible or screen-reader-accessible label — users cannot discover affordance without context. Use Button with icon and text, or pair Icon Button with a tooltip.
basalt.a11y: Icon Button must have aria-label or aria-labelledby; keyboard users navigate via Tab and activate via Enter or Space (implicit button role).

basalt-run--input--field.name: Input Field
basalt-run--input--field.description: Input Field captures single-line text entry with visual states for interaction, validation, and availability.

basalt.usage: Use Input Field for collecting structured data like email addresses, usernames, or search queries where validation feedback is required. Pair with error states when the input fails validation—for example, when a user enters an invalid email format during account signup. Default states with placeholder text guide users toward expected input format without reducing clarity.
basalt.never: Don't use placeholder text as the only label — it disappears on focus and violates WCAG 2.1 Success Criterion 3.3.2. Use a persistent label element paired with placeholder text instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="textbox" (or use semantic <input> element), ensure visible focus indicator with minimum 2px outline, and associate with <label> using matching id and for attributes for screen reader announcement.

basalt-run--menu.name: Menu
basalt-run--menu.description: Menu presents a list of actions or navigation options in a dropdown surface triggered by a button.

basalt.usage: Use Menu when you need to surface secondary actions without consuming persistent layout space, such as sorting options in a table header or account settings in a navigation bar. Menus work well for 3–8 related actions; beyond that, consider a full page or modal. Reserve this component for actions that feel contextual to a specific element.
basalt.never: Don't use Menu to present primary navigation — it buries critical user flows and increases interaction cost. Use navigation components instead.
basalt.a11y: Menu must expose role="menu" on the container, implement keyboard navigation with Arrow Up/Down to move focus between items, and trap focus within the open menu until dismissed with Escape or Tab.

basalt-run--menu--header.name: Menu Header
basalt-run--menu--header.description: Menu Header labels a group of related menu items with a non-interactive, semantic heading.

basalt.usage: Use Menu Header to organize menu sections hierarchically, improving scannability in menus with five or more items. Common in settings panels, navigation menus, and dropdown lists with categorical options—for example, grouping "Workspace" and "Account" sections in a user menu.
basalt.never: Don't make Menu Header clickable or interactive — it breaks semantic meaning and confuses keyboard navigation. Use MenuItem with a disabled state instead.
basalt.a11y: Menu Header must have role="presentation" to prevent screen readers from announcing it as a menu item, preserving the semantic structure of the parent menu.

basalt-run--menu--heading.name: Menu Heading
basalt-run--menu--heading.description: Menu Heading organizes menu items into logical sections with a non-interactive label.

basalt.usage: Use Menu Heading to structure dense menus with multiple categories. In a workspace settings menu with billing, members, and integrations sections, Menu Heading clarifies the hierarchy and helps users locate related options faster. Choose this when sections require visual grouping but no interaction beyond reading.
basalt.never: Don't use Menu Heading as a clickable section toggle — it's a structural label, not a disclosure control. Use Disclosure or Collapsible instead.
basalt.a11y: Menu Heading must have role="heading" with aria-level="3" to establish semantic hierarchy and allow screen reader users to navigate section structure via heading navigation.

basalt-run--menu--item.name: Menu Item
basalt-run--menu--item.description: Menu Item presents a single, actionable choice within a menu or dropdown context.

basalt.usage: Use Menu Item when building a list of related actions within a Menu component, such as file operations in a document editor or account settings in a user dropdown. Reach for this over custom buttons when the action belongs to a set of related options and requires visual grouping.
basalt.never: Don't use Menu Item as a primary navigation element — it's contextual, not structural. Use Navigation or Sidebar instead.
basalt.a11y: Menu Item must have role="menuitem" and support arrow key navigation (ArrowUp, ArrowDown) within its parent Menu with role="menu", per WCAG 1.3.1 (Info and Relationships).

basalt-run--menu--separator.name: Menu Separator
basalt-run--menu--separator.description: Menu Separator visually divides menu items into logical groups using a horizontal line.

basalt.usage: Use Menu Separator to organize related actions within dropdown menus or command palettes. Place separators between distinct action categories—for example, between editing operations and destructive actions in a document editor's context menu. Separators reduce cognitive load by establishing visual hierarchy without requiring additional UI.
basalt.never: Don't use Menu Separator as spacing between every menu item — it diminishes its semantic value and clutters the interface. Use Padding.lg and Padding.sm token spacing instead.
basalt.a11y: Menu Separator must have role="separator" and aria-orientation="horizontal" to communicate its structural purpose to assistive technologies and ensure it is skipped during keyboard navigation.

basalt-run--menu--shortcut.name: Menu Shortcut
basalt-run--menu--shortcut.description: Menu Shortcut displays keyboard command sequences adjacent to menu item labels.

basalt.usage: Use Menu Shortcut to surface keyboard equivalents for frequently-used menu actions, reducing friction for power users. Place it at the trailing edge of a menu item in applications like code editors or design tools where keyboard proficiency directly impacts workflow velocity. Omit shortcuts for uncommon commands or when space is severely constrained.
basalt.never: Don't invent shortcuts that don't actually exist in your application — users will attempt them and encounter failure. Use documented Keyboard Binding instead.
basalt.a11y: Menu Shortcut text must be available to screen readers via aria-label on the parent menu item; the shortcut itself should not be announced redundantly. WCAG 2.1 1.4.5 (Images of Text) — render shortcuts as semantic text, never as images.

basalt-run--navigation--button.name: Navigation Button
basalt-run--navigation--button.description: Navigation Button activates a destination within a hierarchical navigation structure, using directional layout and visual feedback to indicate interactivity and current state.

basalt.usage: Use Navigation Button within sidebar menus, tab bars, or breadcrumb sequences where users navigate between discrete sections of an application. Choose column layout for vertical stacks (sidebar navigation) and row layout for horizontal arrangements (top navigation). The component signals both available destinations and the user's current location through state changes.
basalt.never: Don't use Navigation Button as a call-to-action for primary application tasks — it signals navigation context, not transactional intent. Use Button instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="button" with aria-current="page" on the active destination to satisfy WCAG 2.1 2.4.8 (Location). Keyboard navigation must support arrow keys to move between adjacent buttons and Enter to activate.

basalt-run--navigation--button--list.name: Navigation Button List
basalt-run--navigation--button--list.description: Navigation Button List organizes related navigation actions in a horizontal or vertical container with consistent spacing and visual hierarchy.

basalt.usage: Use this component to group secondary navigation options within a page section or modal, such as filter controls or step progression. Reach for it when you need to establish a clear visual relationship between navigation choices without overwhelming the primary content hierarchy. Common in dashboards, settings panels, and multi-step workflows where related actions benefit from unified styling.
basalt.never: Don't use this component for primary site navigation — use a dedicated Navigation Bar instead. Navigation Button List is for contextual, secondary navigation that serves a specific section or flow, not top-level routing.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="group" on the container with aria-label describing the button list's purpose, and ensure each button child maintains keyboard focus visibility and supports arrow key navigation between items per WCAG 2.1 criterion 2.4.3.

basalt-run--navigation--pill.name: Navigation Pill
basalt-run--navigation--pill.description: Navigation Pill presents a single, self-contained navigation option with distinct visual states.

basalt.usage: Use Navigation Pill when organizing related navigation links within a constrained space, such as filter groups or secondary navigation menus. In a dashboard sidebar, pills efficiently display status filters (Active, Inactive, Archived) without consuming full-width button real estate. Prefer pills over tabs when the navigation context is supplementary rather than primary.
basalt.never: Don't use Navigation Pill for primary page navigation — it lacks the hierarchy and scannability of a full navigation bar. Use Navigation Bar instead.
basalt.a11y: Apply role="button" with aria-pressed="true|false" to communicate selection state. Implement keyboard navigation with arrow keys to cycle between pills and Enter/Space to select, conforming to ARIA Authoring Practices Guide 1.2.

basalt-run--navigation--pill--list.name: Navigation Pill List
basalt-run--navigation--pill--list.description: Navigation Pill List presents a set of mutually exclusive navigation options in a single, compact container.

basalt.usage: Use Navigation Pill List for filtering, view switching, or category selection within a contained context—such as toggling between billing periods, data views, or content categories. This component is ideal when the number of options is fixed and limited (typically 2–5 items). Choose it over tabs when space is constrained or when the selection impacts only the immediate section rather than an entire page.
basalt.never: Don't use Navigation Pill List for more than five options — cognitive load increases and horizontal scroll becomes necessary. Use a Select or Dropdown instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="tablist" on the container with role="tab" on each pill; ensure keyboard navigation via arrow keys and focus management with aria-selected="true" on the active pill (WCAG 2.1 Level AA, 4.1.3 Name, Role, Value).

basalt-run--notification.name: Notification
basalt-run--notification.description: Notification delivers targeted feedback or status information in response to user actions or system events.

basalt.usage: Use Notification to confirm successful operations, warn of potential issues, or inform users of state changes. Deploy Message for confirmations and status updates (e.g., "Payment processed"), and Alert for warnings or errors requiring attention (e.g., "Card declined—update payment method"). Prefer Notification over modals for non-blocking feedback.
basalt.never: Don't stack more than three notifications in the viewport simultaneously — cognitive load increases and earlier messages risk dismissal before being read. Use a notification queue that surfaces one primary notification at a time instead.
basalt.a11y: Apply role="status" (Message) or role="alert" (Alert) to ensure screen readers announce content immediately without interrupting current speech. Set aria-live="polite" for Message and aria-live="assertive" for Alert.

basalt-run--page--accordion.name: Page Accordion
basalt-run--page--accordion.description: Page Accordion organizes lengthy content into collapsible sections, reducing cognitive load while maintaining access to complete information.

basalt.usage: Deploy Page Accordion when a single page contains multiple content sections that don't all require simultaneous visibility—such as FAQ pages, settings panels, or tiered documentation. This component preserves scroll position and allows users to focus on relevant content without horizontal scrolling or page navigation.
basalt.never: Don't nest Page Accordions beyond one level — compound nesting creates keyboard navigation ambiguity and violates WCAG 2.1 Level AA success criterion 2.4.3 (Focus Order). Use a Tabs component or sequential pages instead.
