basalt.a11y: Implement role="region" with aria-labelledby on each accordion panel, aria-expanded on trigger buttons, and ensure all expand/collapse actions are keyboard-accessible via Enter and Space keys per ARIA Authoring Practices Guide 1.2.

basalt-run--page--newsletter.name: Page Newsletter
basalt-run--page--newsletter.description: Page Newsletter captures email addresses through an embedded subscription form with optional headline and supporting copy.

basalt.usage: Deploy Page Newsletter at the end of blog posts, documentation sections, or landing pages to build mailing lists. Use this component when you need a friction-minimal capture mechanism that converts casual readers into subscribers. Common on content-heavy platforms like Vercel's blog or Linear's help center.
basalt.never: Don't auto-focus the email input on page load — it hijacks keyboard navigation and disrupts screen reader users. Use Page Newsletter with explicit user-initiated interaction instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="region" with aria-labelledby pointing to the form headline, and ensure the submit button is reachable via keyboard Tab order with visible focus state per WCAG 2.1 SC 2.4.7.

basalt-run--page--product.name: Page Product
basalt-run--page--product.description: Page Product displays a single product with its details, imagery, pricing, and purchase controls.

basalt.usage: Use Page Product when presenting a complete product view that requires users to evaluate features, compare options, or make a purchase decision. This component serves as the primary interface for e-commerce storefronts, SaaS pricing pages, and marketplace listings where comprehensive product information is essential.
basalt.never: Don't omit status badges for inventory or promotional states — users cannot determine product availability or urgency without this context. Use Status Badge instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="main" on the primary product container and ensure all interactive elements (add to cart, size selectors, quantity controls) meet WCAG 2.1 Level AA contrast ratio of 4.5:1 against their backgrounds.

basalt-run--page--product--results.name: Page Product Results
basalt-run--page--product--results.description: Page Product Results displays a filtered or searched collection of products with pagination and sorting controls.

basalt.usage: Use this component when users need to browse multiple products within a category, search results, or filtered collection. This appears after applying filters in an e-commerce catalog or marketplace. The component manages layout efficiency and information hierarchy across result density and viewport constraints.
basalt.never: Don't display results without a visible sort option — users need agency over result ordering and cannot infer your default ranking. Use the Sorting Control component paired with this layout instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="region" aria-live="polite" on the results container and announce result count changes; results list must support arrow-key navigation between items per WCAG 2.1 level AA.

basalt-run--pagination.name: Pagination
basalt-run--pagination.description: Pagination divides content into discrete pages and enables navigation between them.

basalt.usage: Use pagination when displaying large datasets that would overwhelm a single view, such as search results, table data, or product listings. Pagination improves perceived performance and reduces cognitive load by presenting manageable content chunks. Choose pagination over infinite scroll when users need to reference specific pages or when precise position within a dataset matters.
basalt.never: Don't disable previous or next buttons based on page state without indicating disabled status — users won't understand why interaction failed. Use disabled button states instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="navigation" with aria-label="Pagination" on the container and aria-current="page" on the active page button to establish document structure and current location (WCAG 2.1 1.3.1).

basalt-run--pagination--gap.name: Pagination Gap
basalt-run--pagination--gap.description: Pagination Gap indicates a break in sequential page numbering when listing pages would exceed usable space.

basalt.usage: Use Pagination Gap when page count exceeds available real estate in your pagination component, typically beyond 7-9 pages. Insert between the first/last pages and adjacent numbered pages to compress the display without losing navigation context. For example, a 50-page results set shows pages 1, 2, 3, Gap, 48, 49, 50.
basalt.never: Don't replace Pagination Gap with ellipsis without semantic meaning — screen readers won't announce the skipped range. Use Pagination Gap instead, which properly exposes the number of omitted pages.
basalt.a11y: Pagination Gap must have role="doc-pagebreak" or aria-label="Pages [X] to [Y] omitted" to announce the magnitude of the skip to assistive technology.

basalt-run--pagination--list.name: Pagination List
basalt-run--pagination--list.description: Pagination List navigates between sequential sets of content across multiple pages.

basalt.usage: Use Pagination List when presenting large datasets that need to be distributed across pages, such as search results, data tables, or product listings. This component helps users navigate efficiently without overwhelming a single view. Choose Pagination List over infinite scroll when users need to jump to specific pages or return to previous result sets.
basalt.never: Don't disable previous or next buttons based on page position — users cannot know they've reached a boundary. Use aria-disabled="true" on unavailable page buttons instead, keeping them visible and keyboard-accessible.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="navigation" with aria-label="Pagination" on the container, and ensure each page button uses aria-current="page" for the active page (WCAG 2.1 Level A).

basalt-run--pagination--next.name: Pagination Next
basalt-run--pagination--next.description: Pagination Next advances to the subsequent page within paginated content.

basalt.usage: Use this component when users navigate through multi-page datasets, search results, or documentation. Pair it with Pagination Previous and page indicators to form a complete pagination control. In product listings or table views with 50+ items, pagination next prevents overwhelming single-page renders.
basalt.never: Don't disable pagination next when data exists on the following page — it creates ambiguity about availability. Use Disabled variant only when the current page is definitively the last page.
basalt.a11y: Set aria-label="Next page" and assign role="button" with tabindex="0". Keyboard users must activate via Enter or Space (WCAG 2.1 Level A).

basalt-run--pagination--page.name: Pagination Page
basalt-run--pagination--page.description: Pagination Page renders an individual navigable page number within a pagination control.

basalt.usage: Use Pagination Page when building multi-page result sets, such as search results, data tables, or content feeds. Each page number should be individually selectable and clearly indicate which page the user is currently viewing. In a product listing with 50 results across 5 pages, each page number becomes a Pagination Page component.
basalt.never: Don't disable Pagination Page components to indicate unavailable pages — users cannot distinguish between inactive and interactive states. Use a disabled state token or remove the page number from the pagination control entirely instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role='button' with aria-current='page' on the current page variant and aria-label='Page [number]' on all instances to provide screen reader context (WCAG 4.1.2).

basalt-run--pagination--previous.name: Pagination Previous
basalt-run--pagination--previous.description: Pagination Previous navigates to the preceding page in a paginated result set.

basalt.usage: Use this component when presenting multi-page content like search results, data tables, or transaction histories. Pair it with Pagination Next to form a complete navigation pattern. Place it consistently at the end of page lists or as part of a pagination control group.
basalt.never: Don't disable Pagination Previous on the first page without visual feedback — users need to understand they've reached the beginning. Use Disabled variant instead.
basalt.a11y: Set aria-label to descriptive text such as 'Previous page' and implement keyboard support for Enter and Space keys to activate navigation; WCAG 2.1 level AA requires proper button semantics.

basalt-run--panel--image.name: Panel Image
basalt-run--panel--image.description: Panel Image displays a full-width image container with consistent spacing and background treatment across breakpoints.

basalt.usage: Use Panel Image when presenting hero imagery, campaign visuals, or featured content that anchors a page section. This component is essential in marketing pages, product showcases, and content-heavy layouts where image prominence matters. Choose this over raw img elements to maintain consistent spacing (Space.1200 desktop, Space.600 mobile) and background context (Background.Default.Default with Slate.200 fallback).
basalt.never: Don't nest interactive elements directly over the image without sufficient contrast or touch targets — this violates WCAG 2.1 Level AA color contrast requirements. Use an overlay Panel with explicit hit areas instead.
basalt.a11y: Set role="img" on the parent container and provide descriptive alt text on the img element itself; omit alt text only if the image is purely decorative and marked with aria-hidden="true".

basalt-run--panel--image--content.name: Panel Image Content
basalt-run--panel--image--content.description: Panel Image Content combines a constrained layout container with integrated image display, using semantic spacing and typographic hierarchy to present visual-first information.

basalt.usage: Reach for this component when presenting hero content, feature highlights, or case studies that require equal visual and textual emphasis. Use it in marketing pages, product showcases, or onboarding flows where image credibility directly supports narrative. The component's spacing tokens ensure consistency across viewport scales without manual breakpoint management.
basalt.never: Don't nest multiple Panel Image Content components within a single container — it creates visual confusion and breaks the hierarchy these components establish. Use a grid layout with individual panels instead.
basalt.a11y: Images must include descriptive alt text that conveys meaning independent of adjacent text; implement role="img" on the image container and ensure alt text passes WCAG 2.1 Level AA criterion 1.1.1 for meaningful images.

basalt-run--panel--image--content--rever.name: Panel Image Content Reverse

stories.name: Desktop
stories.snippet: const Desktop = () => <Panel Image Content Reverse variant="desktop" />;

stories.name: Mobile
stories.snippet: const Mobile = () => <Panel Image Content Reverse variant="mobile" />;

basalt-run--panel--image--content--rever.import: import { Panel Image Content Reverse } from "@your-org/design-system";
basalt-run--panel--image--content--rever.description: Panel Image Content Reverse positions an image on the right with text content on the left, creating an asymmetrical layout for highlighting product features or narrative content.

basalt.usage: Use this component when you need to break up vertical monotony in marketing or educational pages while keeping visual hierarchy clear. Deploy it for feature announcements where the image supports but doesn't dominate the message, such as showcasing a product interface alongside its key benefits. Alternate this with standard Panel Image Content to create rhythmic pacing across long-form layouts.
basalt.never: Don't use this component with image-first narratives where the image must be the dominant focal point — use standard Panel Image Content instead. The reverse positioning creates left-to-right reading priority that diminishes visual prominence of right-aligned images.
basalt.a11y: Ensure the image includes a descriptive alt attribute that conveys its purpose independent of adjacent text; WCAG 2.1 1.1.1 requires alternative text for non-decorative images. If the image is purely decorative, mark it with alt="" and set aria-hidden="true" to prevent screen reader announcement.

basalt-run--panel--image--double.name: Panel Image Double
basalt-run--panel--image--double.description: Panel Image Double presents two images side-by-side within a contained layout, typically featuring complementary or comparative visual content.

basalt.usage: Use this component when presenting paired imagery that benefits from direct visual comparison or balanced layout—such as before/after product states, feature comparisons, or related product variants. On product pages and marketing sections, this component creates visual hierarchy while maintaining content density.
basalt.never: Don't place more than two images in a single Panel Image Double instance — this breaks the intended layout balance and creates visual confusion. Use separate Panel Image Double instances or an alternative carousel component instead.
basalt.a11y: Each image must have descriptive alt text that conveys the relationship between the pair. If images are decorative and context is provided by adjacent text, use empty alt attributes (alt="") to prevent redundant announcements (WCAG 1.1.1 Level A).

basalt-run--pricing--card.name: Pricing Card
basalt-run--pricing--card.description: Pricing Card displays plan features, pricing details, and call-to-action in a contained, scannable format.

basalt.usage: Use Pricing Card to present subscription tiers, product editions, or feature comparisons on pricing pages and product selection flows. Each card should represent a distinct offering tier, with the Brand variant highlighting the recommended or most popular option. Cards support inline feature lists, pricing amounts, and primary actions.
basalt.never: Don't use Pricing Card to display product descriptions or marketing copy — cards are for structured plan information only. Use a content section or hero block instead.
basalt.a11y: Each card must have role='article' with an aria-label identifying the plan name (e.g., aria-label='Professional plan, $99 per month'). The primary CTA button must receive keyboard focus in tab order and support Enter/Space activation per WCAG 2.1 Level AA.

basalt-run--product--info--card.name: Product Info Card
basalt-run--product--info--card.description: Product Info Card displays essential product metadata in a compact, scannable container.

basalt.usage: Use Product Info Card when you need to surface product details like pricing, availability, or SKU in catalog views, comparison tables, or detail pages. This component works well in marketplace contexts where users need quick product validation before engagement.
basalt.never: Don't nest interactive elements beyond a single call-to-action button — it creates ambiguous tap targets and splits user attention. Use a dedicated Product Detail Page instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="article" on the root element and ensure the product name uses heading hierarchy (h3 or h4) to maintain outline structure per WCAG 2.1 1.3.1.

basalt-run--radio--field.name: Radio Field
basalt-run--radio--field.description: Radio Field presents a mutually exclusive option within a grouped set, where only one selection is valid at a time.

basalt.usage: Use Radio Field when users must choose exactly one option from a finite set—filtering by status, selecting a shipping method, or choosing a plan tier. Groups should contain two to seven related options; beyond that, prefer Select or Segmented Control for better scanability.
basalt.never: Don't use Radio Fields to toggle a single boolean state — the grouped paradigm creates cognitive friction for binary choices. Use Checkbox or Switch instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="radio" with aria-checked reflecting state, keyboard navigation via arrow keys within the group, and ensure the containing fieldset carries aria-labelledby pointing to a visible legend element (WCAG 1.3.1).

basalt-run--radio--group.name: Radio Group
basalt-run--radio--group.description: Radio Group presents a set of mutually exclusive options where only one selection is possible at a time.

basalt.usage: Use Radio Group when users must choose exactly one option from a predefined set, such as selecting a billing frequency, shipping method, or account tier. Prefer Radio Group over checkboxes when decisions are binary or limited in scope. Reserve it for 2–5 options; beyond that, consider a Select component.
basalt.never: Don't allow multiple simultaneous selections within a Radio Group — it violates the mutually exclusive contract that defines the component. Use Checkbox Group instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="radiogroup" on the container with role="radio" on each option, ensuring keyboard navigation via arrow keys cycles through options and Tab moves focus to the group as a whole (WCAG 2.1 Level A, 2.1.1).

basalt-run--review--card.name: Review Card
basalt-run--review--card.description: Review Card displays aggregated user feedback with star rating and summary text in a contained, scannable format.

basalt.usage: Use Review Card when presenting customer testimonials, product ratings, or feedback summaries that need visual hierarchy and consistent presentation. Common in product detail pages, case studies, or feedback sections where multiple reviews appear in sequence. Choose this when rating data and review text must be equally discoverable.
basalt.never: Don't truncate review text without a clear visual affordance — users cannot distinguish incomplete content from intentional brevity. Use Text.Default.Tertiary for truncation indicators instead.
basalt.a11y: Apply role="article" to the root element and ensure the star rating has aria-label="X out of 5 stars" to convey numeric value to screen readers independent of icon rendering.

basalt-run--search.name: Search
basalt-run--search.description: Search enables users to query and filter content through text input with an optional icon affordance.

basalt.usage: Reach for Search when users need to locate specific items within a dataset—product catalogs, documentation, user lists, or filtered dashboards. Use the default variant for primary search experiences; reserve disabled states for loading or permission-restricted contexts where search is temporarily unavailable.
basalt.never: Don't render Search without a visible label or aria-label — screen reader users need to understand the search context. Use a visually-associated label or consistent aria-label instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="searchbox" on the input element and ensure keyboard navigation supports arrow keys for result navigation and Escape to clear focus (WCAG 2.1 Level AA, 2.1.1 Keyboard).

basalt-run--select--field.name: Select Field
basalt-run--select--field.description: Select Field presents a list of predefined options in a collapsible dropdown, triggered by a single click or keyboard interaction.

basalt.usage: Use Select Field when users must choose one option from a finite, bounded set—such as selecting a country in account settings or filtering a table by status. Prefer this component over text input when options are fixed and discoverable; it eliminates typos and guides users toward valid choices. Avoid for very large lists (100+ items); consider a searchable Combobox or Modal instead.
basalt.never: Don't open the dropdown on focus—open it only on click or Enter key. Opening on focus creates accidental interactions, especially for keyboard users navigating with Tab. Use a Combobox if you need automatic opening behavior.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="listbox" with role="option" on each item, support arrow key navigation (up/down), Enter to select, and Escape to close. Set aria-expanded to reflect open/closed state and aria-selected on the active option.

basalt-run--slider--field.name: Slider Field
basalt-run--slider--field.description: Slider Field captures a single numeric value within a defined range through direct manipulation.

basalt.usage: Reach for Slider Field when users need to select from a continuous range of values, particularly when the approximate position matters more than precise input—such as adjusting volume, brightness, or filtering results by price. Pair with a text input or display value when exact numbers must be visible. Avoid Slider Field for discrete, limited options; use Select instead.
basalt.never: Don't use Slider Field for settings with fewer than 5 meaningful values — the interaction cost outweighs the benefit. Use RadioGroup or Select instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="slider" with aria-valuemin, aria-valuemax, and aria-valuenow attributes. Support arrow key navigation (Left/Right or Down/Up) to increment and decrement values by the appropriate step.

basalt-run--stats--card.name: Stats Card
basalt-run--stats--card.description: Stats Card displays a labeled metric with supporting context in a contained, scannable format.

basalt.usage: Use Stats Card to surface key performance indicators, user achievements, or progress summaries that benefit from visual hierarchy and quick recognition. Common contexts include dashboards presenting conversion rates, analytics pages showing user counts, or settings pages displaying account usage. Stats Card works best when the metric itself is the primary focus and secondary context (like trend direction or comparison) is optional.
basalt.never: Don't use Stats Card for interactive content — it's a read-only container and lacks affordances for user action. Use Button or interactive Input components instead.
basalt.a11y: The metric value must have role="doc-subtitle" or semantically equivalent heading hierarchy, and label text must maintain sufficient contrast ratio (WCAG AA minimum 4.5:1) against Background.Default.Default.

basalt-run--switch--field.name: Switch Field
basalt-run--switch--field.description: Switch Field is a binary control that toggles a single setting between on and off states with accompanying label and description.

basalt.usage: Use Switch Field for boolean preferences that take effect immediately without requiring form submission, such as enabling notifications, toggling dark mode, or activating a feature flag. Prefer it over checkboxes when the action has direct consequences or when the on/off state is the primary focus rather than a selection within a larger form.
basalt.never: Don't use Switch Field to replace radio buttons for mutually exclusive options — the component only represents two states, not selection between multiple alternatives. Use Radio Group instead.
basalt.a11y: Switch Field must use role="switch" with aria-checked set to true or false, and must be fully keyboard accessible via Tab and Space/Enter to toggle state (WCAG 2.1 Level AA).

basalt-run--tab.name: Tab
basalt-run--tab.description: Tab organizes related content into separate views within a single context, allowing users to switch between them without navigating away.

basalt.usage: Use tabs when presenting 2–5 related sections of equal importance, such as different views of the same dataset or account settings categories. Tabs work well in dashboards, settings pages, and detail views where users need quick context-switching without losing their place.
basalt.never: Don't use tabs as a primary navigation pattern — tabs are for switching views within a context, not for moving between major sections. Use a navigation menu or sidebar instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="tablist" on the container, role="tab" on each tab trigger, role="tabpanel" on content sections, and aria-selected="true/false" to reflect active state. Support keyboard navigation with Arrow keys (Left/Right) to switch tabs and Enter to activate, per WCAG 2.1 SC 3.2.3.

basalt-run--tabs.name: Tabs
basalt-run--tabs.description: Tabs organize related content into separate views within a single container.

basalt.usage: Use Tabs to let users switch between different sections without navigating away—common in settings panels, data dashboards, or multi-step forms where all sections are equally important. Prefer Tabs over nested navigation when the user needs to compare or reference content across sections quickly. Reserve Tabs for 2-6 related sections; beyond that, consider a sidebar or sequential steps.
basalt.never: Don't lazy-load tab content to hide loading states — load all tab panels on mount. Use Accordion instead when users need to collapse/expand content or when sections have hierarchical relationships.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="tablist" on the container, role="tab" on each trigger with aria-selected and aria-controls, and role="tabpanel" on each panel. Support keyboard navigation: Arrow Left/Right to switch tabs, Home/End to jump to first/last tab, all with automatic panel activation.

basalt-run--tag.name: Tag
basalt-run--tag.description: Tag organizes content into discrete, scannable categories using color and typography to establish visual hierarchy.

basalt.usage: Use Tag to classify items within lists, filter results, or mark metadata states. Place Tags in product catalogs to surface category membership, in dashboards to denote status or priority, or in forms to show applied filters. Tags reduce cognitive load by replacing longer descriptive text.
basalt.never: Don't use Tag as a button to trigger actions — its passive styling creates confusion about interactivity. Use Button instead.
basalt.a11y: Apply role="note" to tags that convey status or metadata; ensure text contrast meets WCAG AA (4.5:1 minimum). If tags are removable, include aria-label="Remove [tag name]" on the dismiss control.

basalt-run--tag--toggle.name: Tag Toggle
basalt-run--tag--toggle.description: Tag Toggle switches a single categorical filter between active and inactive states.

basalt.usage: Use Tag Toggle when users need to apply or remove a single filter criterion from a set of results—such as toggling product categories in an e-commerce browse flow or enabling/disabling a status filter in a data table. Prefer Tag Toggle over Checkbox when the filter is mutually exclusive within its group or when visual feedback of selection state must be immediate and prominent.
basalt.never: Don't use Tag Toggle to replace a binary yes/no decision in forms — the component signals filtering or categorical selection, not data entry. Use Checkbox or Switch instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="button" with aria-pressed="true|false" to expose toggle state to assistive technology, and ensure keyboard activation via Enter and Space keys per WCAG 2.1 Level AA.

basalt-run--tag--toggle--group.name: Tag Toggle Group
basalt-run--tag--toggle--group.description: Tag Toggle Group presents mutually exclusive or multi-select options as compact, toggleable tags.

basalt.usage: Use Tag Toggle Group to let users filter content by category, status, or attribute within a constrained space. Common in data tables, search results, or sidebar filters where horizontal space is limited and the option set is small (3-8 items). Prefer this over a Select component when all options should remain visible.
basalt.never: Don't use Tag Toggle Group for more than 8 options — the component loses scanability and selection becomes difficult. Use a Select or Radio Group instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="group" on the container and role="radio" or role="checkbox" on each tag (depending on single vs. multi-select). All tags must be keyboard-navigable via arrow keys with Enter/Space to toggle. Ensure the currently selected tag has aria-pressed="true".

basalt-run--testimonial--card.name: Testimonial Card
basalt-run--testimonial--card.description: Testimonial Card displays attributed user feedback with quote, source, and optional metadata in a contained format.

basalt.usage: Use Testimonial Card to showcase customer validation on landing pages, case studies, or product pages where social proof directly influences decision-making. Deploy in carousel layouts or grid sections when featuring multiple perspectives from the same user segment. Prefer this component over inline quotes when attribution and visual hierarchy matter.
basalt.never: Don't truncate testimonial text with ellipsis without a read-more mechanism — users cannot assess credibility from incomplete statements. Use Expandable Card instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="article" on the card container and ensure the quote text has sufficient contrast (WCAG AA minimum) against Background.Default.Default per SC 1.4.3.

basalt-run--text.name: Text
basalt-run--text.description: Text renders semantic body content at medium size with regular font weight.

basalt.usage: Use Text for paragraph copy, descriptions, and inline content where visual hierarchy doesn't demand emphasis. Common contexts include form helper text, list item descriptions, and article body copy. Avoid Text when content requires visual distinction through size or weight; reach for Heading or other semantic variants instead.
basalt.never: Don't use Text for all typographic content — it lacks semantic distinction and creates visual monotony. Use Heading for titles, Subheading for section labels, and Caption for supplementary information instead.
basalt.a11y: Text must render as semantic HTML that matches its visual purpose; use role="doc-paragraph" for extended prose and role="definition" for explanatory content to satisfy WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships.

basalt-run--text--code.name: Text Code
basalt-run--text--code.description: Text Code displays inline code snippets and technical terms with monospace typography.

basalt.usage: Use Text Code for inline code references within prose, API parameters in documentation, or terminal commands in instructional content. It distinguishes code syntax from surrounding text while maintaining reading flow. Reference this component when marking up `userId` parameters or `const` declarations within sentences.
basalt.never: Don't use Text Code for entire code blocks or multi-line snippets — the component lacks the visual containment and context required for complex code. Use Code Block instead.
basalt.a11y: Apply role='code' to ensure screen readers announce the element as code rather than plain text, satisfying WCAG 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value.

basalt-run--text--content--heading.name: Text Content Heading
basalt-run--text--content--heading.description: Text Content Heading pairs a heading with secondary descriptive text in a single, cohesive unit.

basalt.usage: Use this component when a heading requires supporting context or explanation that belongs in close visual proximity. Common in page introductions, section headers with descriptions, or settings panels where a secondary line clarifies purpose. Prefer this over separate heading and paragraph elements when the text forms a unified conceptual block.
basalt.never: Don't stack multiple paragraphs of explanation text — the secondary slot is constrained to single-line content. Use a separate Text component for longer descriptive content instead.
basalt.a11y: Heading must have a semantic heading role (h1–h6) that accurately reflects document outline; secondary text receives role="doc-subtitle" to establish structural relationship per WCAG 1.3.1.

basalt-run--text--content--title.name: Text Content Title
basalt-run--text--content--title.description: Text Content Title pairs a prominent headline with supporting subtitle text, establishing visual hierarchy and semantic structure for page sections.

basalt.usage: Deploy this component at the top of major content sections, landing pages, or feature introductions where a headline-subheadline pairing creates clear narrative entry points. Use it when your content requires both a primary message and contextual explanation—such as a pricing page headline with tier description or a feature section with benefit summary.
basalt.never: Don't nest multiple Text Content Titles vertically without spacing—it creates competing focal points and degrades scanability. Use Space tokens between sequential sections instead.
basalt.a11y: Ensure the title element uses semantic heading tags (h1, h2, h3) matching document outline; subtitle must not be wrapped in a heading tag to preserve logical heading hierarchy per WCAG 2.1 1.3.1.

basalt-run--text--emphasis.name: Text Emphasis
basalt-run--text--emphasis.description: Text Emphasis renders italicized body text at medium size for highlighting secondary information within prose.

basalt.usage: Use Text Emphasis to draw attention to terminology, publication titles, or quoted passages without disrupting reading flow. In a legal document's terms section, italicize defined terms on first mention. Reserve this for contextual highlights—overuse diminishes visual distinction and reading clarity.
basalt.never: Don't use Text Emphasis for interactive elements like links or buttons — italic styling alone fails to signal affordance. Use Link or Button component instead.
basalt.a11y: Ensure italic styling is not the sole means of conveying information; pair with semantic HTML <em> or <i> tags so screen readers can announce emphasis via role="doc-emphasis" or equivalent ARIA markup.

basalt-run--text--heading.name: Text Heading
basalt-run--text--heading.description: Text Heading establishes typographic hierarchy by styling text as a semantic heading element.

basalt.usage: Use Text Heading to mark section introductions, page titles, and content divisions where semantic heading structure matters. In a dashboard, use it for metric category headers that organize related KPIs. Avoid using it for decorative text or when a non-heading element would semantically fit better.
basalt.never: Don't use Text Heading for body content that happens to need larger text — this creates false document structure and breaks screen reader navigation. Use Text or a custom styled component instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement as a native heading element (h1–h6) with role="heading" and aria-level matching document hierarchy to satisfy WCAG 2.4.10 (Page Titled).

basalt-run--text--link.name: Text Link
basalt-run--text--link.description: Text Link navigates users to another page or resource within a single text context.

basalt.usage: Use Text Link when navigation appears inline within body copy, such as within a paragraph, sentence, or list item. It maintains reading flow while signaling interactive affordance. Common in help articles, onboarding flows, and inline documentation where disrupting paragraph structure with a button would create friction.
basalt.never: Don't use Text Link for primary actions — it lacks the visual weight and hit target required for critical user tasks. Use Button instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="link" with href attribute; ensure link text uniquely describes the destination (WCAG 2.1 Level A, criterion 1.3.1) and is distinguishable from surrounding text through color contrast of at least 3:1 against background.

basalt-run--text--link--list.name: Text Link List
basalt-run--text--link--list.description: Text Link List renders a vertical collection of inline text links with consistent spacing and typography.

basalt.usage: Use Text Link List to present related navigation paths, reference materials, or actions of equal importance. In a help center, this component groups related article links; in settings, it organizes interconnected configuration pages. Prefer this over a navigation component when links are contextual content rather than primary navigation.
basalt.never: Don't nest interactive elements inside list items — screen readers will announce redundant roles and create unclear focus targets. Use individual Link components instead, wrapping them in a list structure manually if nesting is required.
basalt.a11y: Implement list semantics with role="list" on the container and role="listitem" on each child; ensure keyboard navigation through list items follows document tab order with each link focusable via Tab.

basalt-run--text--link--list--item.name: Text Link List Item
basalt-run--text--link--list--item.import: import { Text Link List Item } from "@your-org/design-system";
basalt-run--text--link--list--item.description: Text Link List Item renders a navigational link within a vertical list context with semantic meaning and inherited typography.

basalt.usage: Use Text Link List Item when building navigation menus, breadcrumb alternatives, or sidebar link collections where each item deserves individual focus management. Pair multiple instances to create a discoverability pattern, such as a settings sidebar or documentation table of contents. Reserve this component for primary navigation paths rather than inline prose links.
basalt.never: Don't nest interactive elements inside Text Link List Item — screen readers will announce duplicate roles and create focus confusion. Use a separate Button component or restructure the content hierarchy instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="listitem" on the wrapper and ensure the link child has role="link" with href attribute; keyboard focus must be visible and Tab order must reflect document source order (WCAG 2.4.3).

basalt-run--text--list.name: Text List
basalt-run--text--list.description: Text List displays a vertical sequence of related text items with consistent spacing and typographic hierarchy.

basalt.usage: Use Text List to present itemized information where visual separation and semantic structure matter equally—such as feature lists, requirements, or status updates. Choose this component over paragraphs when scannability and vertical rhythm are priorities. Apply it in settings panels, onboarding flows, or documentation where clarity depends on list structure.
basalt.never: Don't use Text List for navigation—semantic meaning requires a nav element with list markup. Use Navigation List instead.
basalt.a11y: Render as an unordered list (ul/li) with role="list" to ensure screen readers announce item count and enable list navigation shortcuts (WCAG 1.3.1).

basalt-run--text--list--item.name: Text List Item
basalt-run--text--list--item.description: Text List Item renders a single line of secondary text at medium weight for listing content hierarchically beneath headings or primary information.

basalt.usage: Use Text List Item when displaying supporting details, metadata, or secondary information in scannable lists. Common contexts include account details beneath user names, file descriptions beneath file names, or status messages beneath actions. Pair with List Item or Container components to establish visual rhythm and proper spacing.
basalt.never: Don't use Text List Item for primary actions or information that requires immediate user attention — it will be visually de-emphasized by design. Use Heading or Text.Default.Primary instead.
basalt.a11y: Text List Item must have role="listitem" when inside a list context (role="list"), and inherit sufficient color contrast from Text.Default.Secondary token which requires WCAG AA contrast ratio of 4.5:1 against its background.

basalt-run--text--price.name: Text Price
basalt-run--text--price.description: Text Price displays monetary amounts with semantic styling and consistent typographic hierarchy.

basalt.usage: Use Text Price when displaying prices, costs, or financial figures that require visual distinction from surrounding content. Deploy in pricing tables, product cards, invoices, or checkout flows where currency amounts need immediate recognition. Select this component over generic text when the monetary value carries primary importance in the interface.
basalt.never: Don't nest Text Price within other typographic components — it manages its own hierarchy through token bindings. Use a dedicated Price component instead.
basalt.a11y: Wrap currency symbols and amounts in a single element with role="doc-noteref" if the price is supplementary, or ensure screen reader users encounter the complete numeric value without abbreviation; test with NVDA to confirm the full amount (including decimals) announces without pause.

basalt-run--text--small.name: Text Small
basalt-run--text--small.description: Text Small renders body text at 12px with regular weight, the smallest typographic scale for supplementary content and UI labels.

basalt.usage: Use Text Small for help text, captions, timestamps, and metadata that provides context without competing for primary focus. In a settings panel, pair it with larger text to explain input requirements. Avoid using it for primary navigation or any content requiring sustained reading.
basalt.never: Don't reduce font size further to fit constrained spaces — visual hierarchy will collapse. Use Text Small itself instead.
basalt.a11y: Maintain a color contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1 (WCAG AA) against its background. At 12px, small text requires higher contrast than larger scales to remain legible.

basalt-run--text--strong.name: Text Strong
basalt-run--text--strong.description: Text Strong renders emphasized text using font-weight 600 at medium body size.

basalt.usage: Use Text Strong for labels, table headers, and short emphasized phrases within body text where semantic emphasis matters. In data tables, apply it to column headers and key row labels to establish visual hierarchy. Avoid using it for full sentences or longer passages—reserve it for words or short phrases that need emphasis.
basalt.never: Don't use Text Strong for entire paragraphs or block-level content — it degrades readability and negates the emphasis effect. Use Heading instead.
basalt.a11y: Text Strong must carry semantic weight via the <strong> element, not CSS alone, to ensure screen readers announce emphasis to users with WCAG 2.1 Level A compliance.

basalt-run--text--subheading.name: Text Subheading
basalt-run--text--subheading.description: Text Subheading renders hierarchical secondary headings with Medium size and configurable font weight and family tokens.

basalt.usage: Use Text Subheading to establish visual hierarchy below primary headings, such as section titles within a settings page or category headers in a product list. This component bridges the gap between body text and main headings, reducing cognitive load when scanning complex information structures.
basalt.never: Don't use Text Subheading for body copy — it carries semantic heading weight (likely h3 or h4 role) and will create noise in the document outline. Use Text.Default instead.
basalt.a11y: Text Subheading must render as a semantic heading element (h2–h6) with role='heading' and aria-level matching its DOM hierarchy depth to maintain a valid document outline per WCAG 1.3.1.

basalt-run--text--subtitle.name: Text Subtitle
basalt-run--text--subtitle.description: Text Subtitle renders secondary-level heading content with a base size and consistent typography foundation.

basalt.usage: Use Text Subtitle for section headers, category labels, or content divisions that establish hierarchy below primary headings. In a settings panel, subtitle components organize related controls. Choose this when you need visual emphasis without the weight of a primary heading.
basalt.never: Don't use Text Subtitle for body content — it's sized and weighted for headlines, not readability at paragraph length. Use Text Body instead.
basalt.a11y: Pair with semantic heading elements (h2–h6) and ensure color contrast meets WCAG AA standard of 4.5:1 against its background.

basalt-run--text--title--hero.name: Text Title Hero
basalt-run--text--title--hero.description: Text Title Hero displays large-scale typographic content with configurable size, weight, and family tokens for prominent page headings and landing page treatments.

basalt.usage: Deploy Text Title Hero for primary page headings, marketing hero sections, and above-the-fold messaging where visual hierarchy demands immediate attention. Use it sparingly—typically once per page—to avoid hierarchy collapse. On a SaaS pricing page, Text Title Hero anchors the main value proposition above supporting copy and CTAs.
basalt.never: Don't nest Text Title Hero within other text components — it establishes typographic baseline and nesting inverts hierarchy logic. Use Heading instead for secondary headings.
basalt.a11y: Bind the component to role="heading" with aria-level="1" to establish page structure and enable screen reader navigation via heading landmarks (WCAG 2.1 1.3.1).

basalt-run--text--title--page.name: Text Title Page
basalt-run--text--title--page.description: Text Title Page displays primary page headings with typography tokens for size, weight, and family.

basalt.usage: Use Text Title Page at the top of major page sections to establish visual hierarchy and content organization. Choose this component for primary headings that introduce distinct content areas, such as dashboard overview pages or documentation landing pages. Pair with supporting text components to create clear information architecture.
basalt.never: Don't use Text Title Page for inline labels or form headers — these demand tighter vertical rhythm and different visual weight. Use Text Label instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="heading" with aria-level="1" to establish semantic document structure and enable screen reader users to navigate page sections via heading shortcuts.

basalt-run--textarea--field.name: Textarea Field
basalt-run--textarea--field.description: Textarea Field captures multi-line text input with optional error states and disabled modes.

basalt.usage: Use Textarea Field for open-ended responses that exceed a single line, such as feedback forms, bug reports, or message composition. Pair with clear labeling and character limits when input length affects downstream processing. Reserve for contexts where the expected response cannot be constrained to predefined options.
basalt.never: Don't use Textarea Field for single-line responses — it creates unnecessary cognitive load and vertical space waste. Use Text Input instead.
basalt.a11y: Textarea Field must have role='textbox' with associated label via aria-labelledby or <label> element; support standard keyboard navigation including Tab, Shift+Tab, and arrow keys within the field without trapping focus.

basalt-run--tooltip.name: Tooltip
basalt-run--tooltip.description: Tooltip displays supplemental information on hover or focus without interrupting the user's workflow.

basalt.usage: Reach for Tooltip to explain icon-only buttons, clarify truncated text, or define unfamiliar terminology. Use it sparingly in dense UIs where space is constrained—such as toolbar actions or data table column headers. Avoid Tooltip for critical information users need before acting; surface that in the interface directly.
basalt.never: Don't place interactive elements inside a Tooltip — they cannot receive focus or keyboard input. Use Popover instead.
basalt.a11y: Implement role="tooltip" and bind it to the trigger with aria-describedby. Tooltips must appear on keyboard focus, not hover alone, to meet WCAG 2.1 Level AA.
